Farn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs LouurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June28,2007
Scott Yuill
State Farm Insurance & Financial Services, Inc.
2221 Sunset Blvd., Suite 111
Rocklin, California 9 57 64
RE
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. ,4.-07-100
Dear Mr. Yuill:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the financial
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the,.Act,,).r
QUESTION
Must you report the fair market value of your S-corporation on your Statement of
Economic Interests (Form 700)?
CONCLUSION
Yes, you must report the fair market value of your S-corporation within one of the
broad value-classes presented in Schedule A-2 of the Form 700.
FACTS
You were elected to the Rocklin City Council in November 2006, and filed your
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700) in February, 2007. You noted on Schedule
A-2 of the Form 700 that you are the owner of a business, Scott Yuill Insurance &
Financial Services, Inc. You reported receiving over $100,000 in income from this
business, but you did not check any of the boxes in Schedul e A-2 to indicate a fair market
value for the business, or the nafure of your investment.2 You were contacted by FppC
staff regarding the missing information, and began discussions that resulted in telephone
I Govemment Code sections 8 I 000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sectiors I 8 109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Schedule A-2 requires that the public official check one of four boxes to indicate an approximate fair
market value for a business activity, within a broad range; $2,000 - $10,000; $10,001 _ $1OO,OOO; $100,001
- $1,000,000; over $1,000,000. The official is also required to state the nature of the investment, which you
omitted to answer, and his or her business position, which you reported as ..owner.,,
F@E


File No. 4-07-100
Page No. 2
advice that you file an amendment to your Form 700 tocure these omissions. you then
sought written advice on your disclosure obligations, including i, d;;;;;;t 
for advice
some additional inform ation.
As we understand the facts now, your business is organized as an S-corporation,
which has several employees. You are the sole owner of the corporation, which you tell
us has "zero assets." You operate the corporation under an agreement with State Farm
Insuralce, which you characteizeas an ai-will personal services contract without resale
value because of its nature as a personal service^s contract. you also believe that any
value the corporation may havels represented by pass{hrough income to you, which you
did report as required
- 
In short, you believe that the nafure of this business is such that it cannot be said
to have any "fair market value," and secondarily you 
"orri"na that if it did have a fair
market value, its gross income would overstate that value "because not recognizing
expenses andlor net profit significantly overestimates the magnitude of thefficy.,,
ANALYSIS
The Act's financial disclosure provisions, Sections 87100 et seq., implement one
of the Act's most important purposes. As stated in section g1003(c): ,.Assets and
income of public officials which may be materially ,ii*t"a by their official actions
should be disclosed and in appropriate circumstanles the officials shouldbe disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.,, This n rau-"rrtal purpose
originates from one of the Act's introductory..Findings and Declarati""r;Grl, 
"t
section 81001(b): .'?ublic officials, whgtfer "r""t"Jtr appointed, should perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias causeJ b, tft. own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported the;.', section g1003 directs that:
"This title should be riberalry construed to^u""ornptirt it, irrposes.,, In other words,
whenever there is any doutt or ambiguitv rl t!" r"gr;;;;f a statute orreguration, the
doubt should be resolved in a manneithat furthers til 6;.*ental purpor3, ortrr" a"t.
The statement of Economic Interests that you have filed is the primary means
whereby potential conflicts of interest may be discovered and averted. A conflict of
interest arises when a goYemmental decision will have u r"u.oruuty foreseeable material
financial effect on ore or more of an official's economic interests. (see generally section
87103)' Identification of an official's economic interests is obviously critical to detection
ofpotential conflicts of interest, but information on the value of these interests is no less
citicaT, because conflicts of interest arise not from trivial or incidental effects, but only
from matertal financial effects on an official,s economic ini"...tr.
The materiality of aneffect on a business entity is measured against the economic
value of the business entity, The expectation is that a reasonabty foreseeable financial
effect of $5,000 would not be "matiial" to a business the size of General Motors, but it
would be "material" to asmall local hardware sto.". r'o.1t i.."u.or, Regulation 1g705.1


File No. ,4.-07-100
Page No. 3
establishes the standards of "mateialit>r'for business entities in a graded series based on
the value of the business from large to small, with the mateialitytLeshold diminishing
with the value of the business. when a public official does not disclose the value of a
business in which he or she has an economic interest, it is not possible to know whether a
foreseeable financial effect is material, and the "rr"niia fo.pose of disclosure is defeated.
You argue that your corporation has no assets, and no ..fair market value,, because
its principle asset, your personal relationship with stat" ear- and your clients, cannot be
sold' since your corporation has several employees -a ir. revenues suggest a substantial
volume of business, the corporation may have tangibi" ,s"t. like a real property lease
and office furnishings and equipment, whose fair irarket values .*."udivie calcutated.
But even if there are no tangible assets, ye think that you are mistaken in your belief that
its great intangible asset, your relationship with State Farm and your clients, has no ,.fair
market value," especially when that termls hberally construed to further the purposes of
the Act's disclosure provisions.
Intangible assets like the one you describe are characteristic of manyprofessional
business enterprises, rike medical, dental o. u""orrrtirg [i"ti""r, as welr as danchises,
all of which are conlmonly sord to quarified p."f.;;i;;ri;, and whos e,,fairmarket value,,
is often quantified for other p.rtpo."t such as-marital dissolutions or estate planning. The
IRS indeed presumes that "personal services corporatio.rrlj t urr" a fair marliei valu", for
example in its Publication 542. Aprofessional 'ibook oitrrrirr"r.,, and the associated
goodwill that generates six-figure annual incomes *" rror*ithout a determinable value.
The Form 700 does not requiri a precise valuation; it is only necessary to check a box
indicating the broad range within whi"lrh: value or, purti."lar business falls, such as
$100,001 - $1,000,000. If you are unable by yorrr"tiri *ir" at a reasonable estimate of
your corporation's fair market value, you can enlist the services of a quain"Juusiness
appraiser for assistance in this regard.
Finally, we note that you have also failed to indicate the,Nafure of Investment,,
on schedule A-2' Because you do not reference any aim""rtv with this i;;;. 
believe
this to be an inadvertent omission. Frol our understanding of the facts, you can cure this
omission by checking the box rabered..other,,, *a ritiigin..S_corporation.,,
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
f*r-,- I
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must you report the fair market value of your S-corporation on your Statement of
Economic Interests (Form 700)?

CONCLUSION: Yes, you must report the fair market value of your S-corporation within one of the
broad value-classes presented in Schedule A-2 of the Form 700.
